Skewed distributions are asymmetrical with most scores "piled up" in one tail of the distribution and a few scores in the other tail: In a negatively skewed distribution, the few scores are in the negative tail (the low end of the distribution); in a positively skewed distribution, the few scores are in the positive tail (the high end of the distribution) - i.e., the "tail tells the tale." In both types of skewed distributions, the mean, median, and mode do not equal the same value: Instead, the mean is in the tail with the few scores, the median is in the middle, and the mode is in the tail containing most of the scores. Consequently, in a negatively skewed distribution, the mean is the lowest score, the median is the middle score, and the mode is the highest score. Conversely, in a positively skewed distribution, the mean is the highest score, the median is the middle score, and the mode is the lowest score.

A tort is a civil wrongdoing that results from a negligent breach of duty that results in injury or harm to a patient. Torts can be intentional or unintentional. Conversion (theft of or damage to personal property), infliction of emotional distress, and fraud/deceit are types of intentional torts. Unintentional torts (e.g., malpractice) involve negligence and are also known as negligence torts. Being familiar with the four conditions required for a claim of malpractice would have helped you identify the correct answer to this question: (a) The psychologist must have had a professional relationship with the patient that established the psychologist's duty to conform to a professional standard of care. (b) There was a breach of the duty (i.e., negligence) on the part of the psychologist. (c) The patient suffered injury or harm as a result of this breach. (d) The psychologist's breach of duty was the direct or proximate cause of the patient's harm or injury.

subdomain_name: APA Ethics Code Overview and Standards 1 and 2
chapter_title: Drawing the Line and Crossing It Ethically: Competence Boundaries in Professional Practice
verbatim: Hiring psychology graduate students to administer measures is acceptable as long as the students are provided with training in administering the measures and relevant ethical guidelines.

verbatim: Psychologists may prioritize ethical responsibilities or legal requirements, depending on the nature of the situation, when facing unresolvable conflicts between their ethical responsibilities and legal requirements, according to the ethics codes of the American and Canadian Psychological Associations.

subdomain_name: APA Ethics Code Overview and Standards 1 and 2
chapter_title: Drawing the Line and Crossing It Ethically: Competence Boundaries in Professional Practice
verbatim: To be consistent with ethical requirements, a psychologist who specializes in treating depression but has not worked with African American or low-income clients should agree to see a new client from those populations as long as they consult with a colleague who has experience working with those clients.

subdomain_name: APA Ethics Code Overview and Standards 1 and 2
chapter_title: The Ethical Architecture: How Professional Standards Protect Psychologists and the Public

chapter_title: Drawing the Line and Crossing It Ethically: Competence Boundaries in Professional Practice
verbatim: To be consistent with ethical requirements, a psychodynamic psychotherapist with limited experience in mindfulness meditation should inform the client of their limited experience, discuss the client's options, and refer the client to a qualified therapist if they continue to want training in mindfulness meditation.

subdomain_name: APA Ethics Code Overview and Standards 1 and 2
chapter_title: Applied Ethics in Context: Navigating Violations, Conflicts, and Cultural Complexity

subdomain_name: APA Ethics Code Overview and Standards 1 and 2

subdomain_name: APA Ethics Code Overview and Standards 1 and 2

chapter_title: Applied Ethics in Context: Navigating Violations, Conflicts, and Cultural Complexity

chapter_title: Standard 4 Applied: Minors, Posthumous Records, Electronic Communication, and Organizational Contexts

verbatim: Privilege is a legal concept that protects a client's confidential information from being disclosed in legal proceedings.

verbatim: The holder of the privilege is ordinarily the client or the client's legal representative.

verbatim: Open access is most consistent with the General Principle of Justice in the APA's Ethics Code, which calls for fairness and justice to entitle all persons to access to and benefit from the contributions of psychology.

verbatim: This practice is unethical since the intern should be identified as the person who administered and scored the tests.

chapter_title: The Ethical Architecture: How Professional Standards Protect Psychologists and the Public
verbatim: Psychologists do not have to obtain a client's authorization before discussing the client's case with a consultant if they can disguise the client's identity while doing so, according to the ethical guidelines of the American and Canadian Psychological Associations.

chapter_title: Standard 3 Applied: Forensic Ethics, Informed Consent, and the Limits of Disclosure
verbatim: When a forensic psychologist is hired to conduct a psychological evaluation of a defendant in a criminal case, the "client" is the person/entity that retained the psychologist.

chapter_title: Standards 5-8: Advertising, Fees, Records, Research, and Education Ethics
verbatim: Inviting Annie's husband to participate in Annie's therapy sessions for her social phobia is acceptable since the psychologist believes his participation will benefit Annie, as it is consistent with the standard that prohibits uninvited solicitation but allows attempting to implement appropriate collateral contacts for the purpose of benefiting an already engaged therapy client.

chapter_title: Standards 5-8: Advertising, Fees, Records, Research, and Education Ethics
verbatim: To be consistent with ethical requirements, psychologists can solicit testimonials from former (but not current) clients as long as they're not vulnerable to undue influence.

Withholding a client's record for nonpayment of fees is always unethical when the record is needed for the client's emergency treatment.

subdomain_name: APA Ethics Code Overview and Standards 1 and 2
chapter_title: Applied Ethics in Context: Navigating Violations, Conflicts, and Cultural Complexity
verbatim: When psychologists are faced with conflicts between ethical responsibilities and legal requirements, they may comply with legal requirements when doing so doesn't violate basic human rights.

chapter_title: Standards 5-8: Advertising, Fees, Records, Research, and Education Ethics
verbatim: When confidential information is faxed or otherwise transferred electronically, it is not possible to guarantee it will not be accessed by unauthorized individuals, so the best course of action is to remove all identifying information before faxing the requested information to the therapist.
As an ethical psychologist, you should discuss the client's concerns and tell her that you need to keep a record of her sessions and explain your reasons for doing so.
As an ethical psychologist, in-person solicitation of business may be acceptable if it is uninvited as long as it does not target people who are vulnerable to undue influence.

It is unacceptable for Dr. Lansky to list her Ph.D. in business administration on her business card and advertisements for her clinical psychology practice, as it does not meet the requirements of the APA's Ethics Code and the Canadian Code of Ethics.

chapter_title: Standard 3: Navigating Human Relations, Multiple Relationships, and the Duty to Avoid Harm
verbatim: A psychologist should refuse to conduct a child custody evaluation for a former therapy client, as this would constitute a multiple relationship.

chapter_title: Standards 5-8: Advertising, Fees, Records, Research, and Education Ethics
verbatim: Accepting contingent fees should usually be avoided, as the APA Ethics Code and Specialty Guidelines for Forensic Psychology state that the threat to impartiality presented by the acceptance of contingent fees should lead forensic practitioners to strive to avoid providing professional services on the basis of contingent fees.

chapter_title: Standards 5-8: Advertising, Fees, Records, Research, and Education Ethics
verbatim: APA's Record Keeping Guidelines recommend retaining the full client record until 7 years after the last date of service delivery for adults or until 3 years after a minor reaches the age of majority, whichever is later.

Using a collection agency to collect unpaid fees is acceptable only if the psychologist notifies the client of their intent and provides the client an opportunity to make prompt payment before doing so.
Psychologists can ethically place paid advertisements in newspapers as long as the columns are clearly identified as such.

This situation is directly addressed in Standard 5.04 of the APA Ethics Code and indirectly addressed in Standard III.5 of the Canadian Code of Ethics, where the psychologist would want to make sure that callers understand that a professional therapeutic relationship is not established when she responds to their questions and that the advice or opinions she provides do not constitute therapy.

chapter_title: Standard 3 Applied: Forensic Ethics, Informed Consent, and the Limits of Disclosure

chapter_title: Standards 5-8: Advertising, Fees, Records, Research, and Education Ethics
verbatim: Agreeing to the arrangement where Mrs. Giovanni exchanges home cleaning services for therapy would be unethical, as it creates a potentially harmful multiple relationship.
Routinely waiving insurance co-payments without the insurance company's agreement constitutes insurance fraud and is unethical and illegal.
Psychologists are prohibited from soliciting testimonials from current clients and, in some circumstances, former clients.

Unless a psychologist clearly communicates that responding to viewer emails and calls does not establish a therapeutic relationship, accepting an offer to present mental health information on a TV program would be unethical.
A psychologist starting a private practice may offer the first therapy session for free to new clients if she clearly specifies her usual hourly fee and the purpose of the first session.

chapter_title: Standards 5-8: Advertising, Fees, Records, Research, and Education Ethics
verbatim: A psychologist may refuse to forward a client's record to a colleague for nonpayment of fees only if the records are not needed for the client's emergency treatment.

To be consistent with ethical requirements, a psychologist will notify the client that they will contact a collection agency if the client does not pay for their services as agreed and does not respond to the psychologist's letters asking them to contact the psychologist to discuss the unpaid bill.
Requiring psychology students to participate in individual or group therapy may be acceptable if they are allowed to select providers from mental health professionals not affiliated with the program.

chapter_title: Standards 5-8: Advertising, Fees, Records, Research, and Education Ethics
verbatim: It is acceptable for psychologists to receive a fee from referral of clients to another psychologist only if the fee represents the psychologists' actual costs for referring the clients.

Changing the format of the presentation from a "lecture" to having attendees describe their personal experiences with eating disorders is unethical because it violates the requirements of the APA Ethics Code and Canadian Code of Ethics, which require psychologists to provide accurate descriptions of the content of the programs they provide.
Bartering may be acceptable according to the ethics codes of the American and Canadian Psychological Associations when it is not clinically contraindicated.
Psychologists should reach an agreement with new clients about fees and other financial issues as early as is feasible in the professional relationship.
Billing an insurance company for a reimbursable form of therapy instead of the non-reimbursable form that's actually being provided is unethical.
Psychologists are prohibited from becoming sexually involved with students over whom they have or are likely to have evaluative authority.

A graduate program in clinical psychology requires first-year students to participate in individual therapy, which is acceptable as long as there are no multiple relationships between the students and the therapists according to the APA Ethics Code and the Canadian Code of Ethics.

Psychologists must allow research participants, who are students receiving class credit, to withdraw from a study at any time during the study, as required by the APA Ethics Code and Canadian Code of Ethics.
Dr. Merritt's evaluation of Alice is not acceptable because it was not based entirely on Alice's actual performance as an intern.
Billing insurance companies for missed appointments without their approval would be insurance fraud, which is both unethical and illegal.
Sexual relationships with supervisees over whom psychologists have evaluative authority are prohibited according to the ethics codes of the American and Canadian Psychological Associations.

If a psychologist's former intern is not likely to be under the psychologist's evaluative authority in the future, a sexual relationship between them is considered acceptable according to ethical guidelines.

verbatim: A professional executor may be a licensed psychologist or other licensed health provider.

Offering inducements to research participants, such as a gift card to a local restaurant, is acceptable as long as it is not likely to coerce participation.

The APA Ethics Code and Canadian Code of Ethics require psychologists to allow research participants to withdraw from a study at any time, so an ethical psychologist would allow the students to withdraw from the study.
This is acceptable as long as the client's complaint is valid and Dr. Devlin has previously provided the intern with feedback about his performance.

If a research psychologist decides it's not possible to conduct a study without deceiving participants about the true purpose, they must tell participants about the true purpose as early as feasible and allow them to withdraw their data.
Use of deception in research may be acceptable when alternative procedures are not available and subjects are informed about the deception "as early as is feasible."

Debriefing research participants about the nature and results of a research study is required, but in some cases, debriefing may not be necessary if scientific or humane values justify delaying or withholding this information.

The 7th edition of the Publication Manual of the American Psychological Association states that authors should retain raw data associated with their articles published in APA journals in accordance with institutional or funder requirements.

According to the ethical requirements for authorship of a journal article based substantially on a student's doctoral dissertation, the student must be listed as the principal author, except under exceptional circumstances.

It would be unacceptable for the post-doctoral student to list one of the professors in her department as the principal author of the article on the research study she has just completed, as the professor's contribution was relatively minor.

Covering only research on aging that produces results similar to one's own studies in an introductory neuropsychology class would be unacceptable, as it violates ethical requirements to present instructional information in an accurate and unbiased manner.

This issue is addressed in Standard 7.04 of the APA's Ethics Code and the Values Statement for Standard I and Standard I.8 of the Canadian Code of Ethics. Standard 7.04 states that students should not be required to disclose personal information in course- or program-related activities related to psychological treatment except in two circumstances: (a) this requirement has been clearly identified in program and admissions materials or (b) the information is needed to "evaluate or obtain assistance for students whose personal problems could reasonably be judged to be preventing them from performing their training- or professionally related activities in a competent manner or posing a threat to the student or others."

If Dr. Wendy Wundt terminates Lloyd's internship due to his recent behaviors, she will have acted ethically only if she first provides Lloyd with a plan to remediate his unacceptable behaviors and Lloyd continues to engage in those behaviors.

Simultaneous (concurrent) submission of an article to multiple journals is unacceptable, as the author(s) should submit the article to one journal at a time and wait for it to be rejected by one journal before submitting it to another journal.

In his dissertation proposal, a graduate student used the organization and headings used by the author of a published journal article, and used arguments and data presented by that author and the author of another published article on the same topic to justify his study's hypotheses, but he cited both authors only once in the introductory paragraph, which is unacceptable since the graduate student has not given adequate credit to the other authors.

Except under exceptional circumstances, the student should be listed as principal author on any multiple-authored article that is substantially based on the student's doctoral dissertation.
